titleStmt.title: Letter from Daulat Ram Kalia, March 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Daulat Ram Kalia

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a letter from Daulat Ram Kalia, an Indian student. Kalia is writing in the hope that his letter will be brought to the attention of the Viceroy of Ireland, Sir Ivor Churchill Guest, Lord Wimbrone (1873-1939). Kalia was writing to complain about the newspaper 'The Eye-Opener'. The paper caused some controversy due to an article it published with the heading 'Black Peril' which attacked Indian students. The paper claims that 'coloured people' were savage and that any white person seen in their company was a disgrace. Kalia asks the lord lieutenant to act against the offensive paper.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/5575/4

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Dear Sir, I shall feel much obliged
if you will care to lay the following lines
before His Excellency the Viceroy of Ireland. 1. The Eye- Opener, a recently started newspaper
of Dublin, has in its two last issues
written on the 'Black peril' in Dublin.
The writings are marked in the enclosed
copies & speak for themselves. 2. The general impression of the language
on an unprejudiced mind is that it is
calculated to produce feelings of ill-
will & hatred between too classes of His
Majesty's subjects - in the white & the
coloured people. The Dundrum put on
page 3rd of the issue of 25.3.16 considers
it a disgrace for a white person to be seen
in the company of a coloured one. Not
only this but the words of the said
publication on Friday last had the
words 'Black Peril' on the in glowing
& big type & thus provoking every coloured man. 3. The publication in question really offended
the coloured class so for that they held
a meeting on Saturday last to consider
what measures should be taken
to put a stop to such offensive and
provocative language as has been
offending in the said publication. In
the meeting the indignation was
great & surely the wounded feelings
seemed to hope for a remedy. 4. It is also worth mentioning that the
'abundance of coloured gentlemen' finds
its only interpretation in the incidence
of the Indian students here, who all want to
come to a considerable result. 5. It is simply shocking to see that
there law abiding subjects of His Majesty
are being labelled with such references
which are nothing short of an attempt
to outrage their feelings of decency. If they
deserved anything here it was sympathy
& hospitality so that they had left this
beautiful town with happy memories &
not an abusive language like the one
under influence. 6. The Govt: of India is doing its best to prompt
feelings of sympathy between the rulers
& the ruled & of everything possible is being
done to bring about cordial relations
between the Europeans & the Indians. In
London where the Indian students are
found in hundreds the Govt: is looking
after their comforts, amusements the
religion & the students go back to
their country full of the feelings of gratitude
towards their foreign rulers. Instead
of of anything of the kind the paper in question
has thought fit to hold them up to ridi-
cule, contempt and hatred. The editor may
have his motives, doubt or distrust he
knows well, but he should remain
within the bounds of law & refrain
from using language which incites a community
as a whole. Who would on earth think
that every meeting of a coloured gentleman
& a white lady is for unmoral purposes! Does
the editor want that they their should be un
social influence between two classes of
His Majesty's subjects? Is it consistent
with the policy of the European which
has made its protection such a
namely of races! Surely the editor
is doing a mischief which is doing an
incalculable harm & in his guise to
attempt to play a social reformer he
is offending & wounding the feelings
of a race which comes to about 3/4 of
His Majesty's subjects. Again in view
of what India is doing for the war &
in the interest of the Empire it is
hardly admirable that those responsible
for the running of the 'eye -opener' should
be allowed to condemn us with impunit-
y. 7. The writing in question in my humble
opinion amounts to seditious libel on
a class (coloured class as opposed to white)
& it is hoped His Excellency will
surely take steps in the matter-
8. During the regime of Lord Hardinge
in India as towns were formed by the
Govt: to establish wholesome relations
between the rulers & the ruled & Lord
Hardinge's name has become a
household word in India for his
sympathy & love for the Indians. In
countenance of the same token we
bring hope that - Lord Chelmsford
our new viceroy will be able to migrate
with his generous policy the plant which
Lord Hardinge has sown there. His Excellency
Lord Wimborne being related to our
new viceroy we hope that he wanted
to look to this just complaint of
the subject consented to his brother in law
which has been made to him
in the interest of justice & good
administration & above all
in the interest of the European
one which the sun does not
set set. With deepest respect - to His Excellency
of Ireland.
Your very obedient servant D.R.Kalia.
